name: APT28 Fancy Bear Lateral Movement Campaign
description: A sophisticated lateral movement campaign modeled after APT28 (Fancy Bear / Sofacy) tactics. The attack begins with a compromised developer workstation via a trojanized Python package (supply chain compromise), followed by credential dumping with Mimikatz, Active Directory reconnaissance with BloodHound, lateral movement using PsExec and WMI, Golden Ticket persistence, and ultimately domain-wide compromise. This mirrors real APT28 TTPs documented in campaigns against government and defense organizations.

phases.name: Supply Chain Compromise via Trojanized Python Package
phases.description: A developer installs a trojanized Python package 'python-dateutil-x' (typosquatting the legitimate python-dateutil). The malicious package contains a post-install script that downloads and executes the XAgent backdoor (APT28's custom implant). The backdoor establishes a reverse HTTPS connection to the C2 server. This mirrors real supply chain attacks documented against PyPI in 2023.
phases.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain

expected_logs.description: pip install triggers setup.py with embedded PowerShell downloader
python.exe spawns powershell.exe -EncodedCommand
HTTPS connection to C2 at 194.36.209.100 on port 443
XAgent binary dropped to %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\svchost.exe
Scheduled task created for persistence

phases.name: Credential Dumping with Mimikatz
phases.description: APT28 uses Mimikatz to dump credentials from LSASS process memory on the developer workstation. They extract NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and cleartext passwords. The attacker uses a reflective DLL injection technique to load Mimikatz directly into memory to avoid disk-based detection, a well-documented APT28 evasion technique.

expected_logs.description: Suspicious process accessing lsass.exe memory
PowerShell Invoke-Mimikatz reflective loading
Sensitive privilege use: SeDebugPrivilege enabled
Credential Manager accessed
Reflective DLL injection into legitimate process

phases.name: Active Directory Reconnaissance with BloodHound
phases.description: APT28 deploys SharpHound (BloodHound's data collector) to map the entire Active Directory environment, identifying the shortest attack path from the compromised developer account to Domain Admin. They discover that jean.martin has GenericAll rights on a Group Policy Object linked to the Domain Controllers OU, providing a path to domain dominance.

expected_logs.description: SharpHound.exe executed with -c All collection method
High volume LDAP queries to domain controller srv-ad-01
net group /domain and nltest /dclist enumeration
BloodHound ZIP output created in C:\Users\Public\
Kerberos service ticket requests for multiple SPNs (Kerberoasting recon)

indicators.attack_path_identified: jean.martin -> GenericAll on GPO -> Domain Controllers OU -> Domain Admin

phases.name: Lateral Movement via PsExec and WMI
phases.description: Using the NTLM hashes obtained from Mimikatz, APT28 performs Pass-the-Hash attacks to move laterally to the web server (srv-web-01) using Impacket's psexec.py and wmiexec.py. They deploy additional persistence mechanisms on each compromised host. The attacker avoids using RDP to reduce forensic artifacts, consistent with documented APT28 tradecraft.

expected_logs.description: NTLM authentication from ws-dev-01 using Pass-the-Hash
Admin share access: \\srv-web-01\ADMIN$ and \\srv-web-01\C$
PsExec service binary PSEXESVC.exe installed and started
cmd.exe spawned by PSEXESVC service
WMI connection over DCOM (port 135) from ws-dev-01

phases.name: Web Shell Deployment and Persistence
phases.description: APT28 deploys a custom ASPX web shell (a variant of their documented tool 'XTunnel') on the web server for persistent access independent of the C2 channel. The web shell is obfuscated and placed in a legitimate-looking IIS directory. Additionally, they create a Golden Ticket using the krbtgt hash obtained from the domain controller to ensure persistent domain access.

expected_logs.description: New ASPX file created in IIS web root
w3wp.exe spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe
HTTP POST requests to /iishelp/maintenance.aspx
Kerberos TGT forged with anomalous lifetime (Golden Ticket)
Account logged on with forged Kerberos ticket

phases.name: Domain Controller Compromise via DCSync
phases.description: APT28 performs a DCSync attack against the domain controller using the compromised domain admin credentials, replicating the entire Active Directory database including all user password hashes. They then pivot to the database server (srv-db-01) and dump sensitive application data. This phase represents full domain compromise, consistent with APT28's documented objective of total environment control.

expected_logs.description: Directory service replication request from non-DC source
MS-DRSR (Directory Replication Service) traffic from ws-dev-01
Privileged logon with Domain Admin from developer workstation
SQL queries extracting sensitive data from application database
New domain admin account created for persistence

expected_detection_points: Python/pip spawning PowerShell with encoded commands
Process accessing lsass.exe memory (Mimikatz credential dumping)
Kerberoasting via excessive Kerberos TGS requests
Pass-the-Hash NTLM authentication from developer workstation
PsExec service installation on remote hosts (PSEXESVC)
Web shell deployment in IIS web root
Golden Ticket with anomalous Kerberos ticket lifetime
DCSync replication request from non-domain controller
